title: Purple or Black Teat in Nursing Cats: Mastitis Guide
meta_desc: If your nursing cat has a purple or black teat, it may be a life-threatening emergency. Learn about gangrenous mastitis and when to see a vet.
content_html:
What is it?

Purple or black discoloration on a nursing cat's mammary gland is often a sign of gangrenous mastitis. Mastitis is an infection of the breast tissue that occurs when bacteria enter the teat canal. When the infection becomes severe, it can cut off the blood supply to the tissue, causing the skin and underlying gland to die (necrosis), which results in a dark purple or black appearance.

Is it an emergency?

Yes. The urgency level is High. If a teat has turned purple or black, the infection has progressed to a critical stage. This condition is extremely painful and can quickly lead to septic shock or systemic organ failure if the bacteria and toxins enter the cat's bloodstream. You should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately for treatment.

Why a photo helps triage

Taking a clear, well-lit photo of the affected teat can be incredibly helpful for a veterinary triage team. A photo allows the medical staff to assess the extent of the tissue death and inflammation before you even arrive. This helps them prepare the necessary surgical or intravenous treatments in advance, potentially saving vital time in an emergency situation.

faqs.question: Can the kittens still nurse if the mother has a black teat?
faqs.answer: No. You must prevent kittens from nursing from any affected glands. The milk in an infected gland can contain dangerous bacteria and toxins that could be fatal to the kittens. Additionally, the process is incredibly painful for the mother.

faqs.question: What are other symptoms of severe mastitis in cats?
faqs.answer: In addition to discoloration, you may notice the teat feels hot, hard, or swollen. The cat may also show signs of fever, lethargy, loss of appetite, or may cry out when the kittens attempt to nurse.

faqs.question: How is gangrenous mastitis treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment typically requires hospitalization, intravenous antibiotics, pain management, and often surgical removal of the dead (necrotic) tissue to prevent the spread of infection.
